source: Gangs of Wasseypur — Part 1 (2012) — script (Hindi dialogue did not text-extract from the available dub PDF; iconic lines grounded via documented reception/quotes rather than the script file)
love_type: cult — moderate theatrical performance, then a massive enduring cult on streaming/memes built on its dialogue, swagger and music; the definitive modern Hindi cult film
core_emotion: the intoxicating thrill of swagger and vengeance — small-town menace made operatic, profane and darkly funny

dialogue_style.register: raw, profane, hyper-local Bihar/Dhanbad dialect — abusive, earthy, rhythmic; gangsters who talk like real men of the coal belt, not movie-stars
dialogue_style.rhythm: throwaway menace and casual obscenity that suddenly crystallize into a chillingly calm vow
dialogue_style.stylization: gritty realism shot through with absurdist black comedy — violence and farce in the same breath
dialogue_style.why_it's_theirs: the authenticity of the cuss-laden vernacular is the whole brand — it sounds overheard, not written, which is why fans quote it endlessly

signature_dialogues.line: Keh ke lenge / Keh ke lunga.
signature_dialogues.construction: a boast of premeditated revenge — 'I'll tell him before I take it'; menace through restraint, the threat made worse by announcing it
signature_dialogues.the_setup: Sardar Khan vows vengeance against Ramadhir Singh for his father's death; he won't strike in secret, he'll do it openly
signature_dialogues.presentation: delivered calmly, repeated across the saga as a refrain so it becomes the character's signature stamp; the calm announcement of violence is scarier than rage, and the repetition makes it a brand
signature_dialogues.payoff: turns revenge into a declared, inevitable promise hanging over the whole epic
signature_dialogues.why_quotable: entered everyday Hindi as cocky shorthand for 'I'll get you, openly' — endlessly memed

signature_dialogues.line: Goli nahi maarenge, keh ke lenge uski.
signature_dialogues.construction: antithesis — not a bullet, but a declared humiliation; reframes revenge as something slower and more total than killing
signature_dialogues.the_setup: Sardar explains his method of vengeance against the man who wronged his family
signature_dialogues.presentation: spoken as cold strategy, not heat — the matter-of-factness is the menace; staged as a quiet vow that the rest of the film then executes
signature_dialogues.payoff: establishes the character's terrifying patience and the saga's engine of generational revenge
signature_dialogues.why_quotable: the 'won't shoot, will announce' inversion is the line fans love to repeat

signature_dialogues.line: Sardar Khan naam hai humara. Bata dijiyega sabko.
signature_dialogues.construction: self-introduction as power move — naming oneself as a threat to be broadcast; swagger compressed to an order
signature_dialogues.the_setup: Sardar announces himself to his enemies' world
signature_dialogues.presentation: delivered with absolute calm bravado, a man branding his own legend — the confidence is the spectacle
signature_dialogues.payoff: mythologizes the character in his own words; the audience adopts the swagger
signature_dialogues.why_quotable: a portable flex anyone can deploy; the self-mythologizing cadence is irresistible

the_peaks.moment: Sardar Khan's calm revenge vow against Ramadhir Singh
the_peaks.why_loved: menace through restraint — the coolest possible way to promise violence; pure swagger

the_peaks.moment: the relentless, blackly comic cycle of betrayal and bullet across generations
the_peaks.why_loved: operatic small-town crime epic that treats vengeance as folklore — endlessly rewatchable texture

wish_fulfillment: raw, uninhibited power and defiance — saying and taking exactly what you want with total swagger and no fear of consequence
character_magnetism: Sardar Khan: profane, fearless, magnetic — a gangster so charismatic his menace reads as cool; the whole rogues' gallery is quotable
audacity: an 5-hour-plus profane crime epic with no glamour, antiheroes top to bottom, violence and farce fused — refusing every mainstream Bollywood comfort and winning a cult for exactly that
portability: 'Keh ke lunga' and 'Tumse na ho payega' (Part 2) became everyday Hindi phrases and meme staples; the music and swagger are endlessly sampled
why_cult_not_just_good: its dialogues and characters live as a self-sustaining meme/quote economy online — fans speak in its lines; the film became a vocabulary
transferable_love_triggers: Make menace CALM — announce violence quietly and matter-of-factly; restraint is scarier and cooler than rage, and far more quotable.
Give a character a repeated verbal stamp (a refrain like 'keh ke lunga') so it becomes a brand the audience adopts as their own swagger.
Write dialogue that sounds OVERHEARD, not authored — hyper-local profane vernacular reads as authentic and gets quoted precisely because it feels real.
Build revenge as a declared, inevitable promise hanging over the story rather than a surprise — the announced threat is the engine and the catchphrase.
Fuse violence with farce in the same breath — the tonal audacity is what makes it rewatchable and meme-able rather than merely grim.
confidence: high on the iconic lines and cult status (documented); NOTE: the supplied PDF's Hindi dialogue did not text-extract, so quotes are grounded via reception sources, not the script file
